output.title: Landlord Withholding Security Deposit? Your Fast Recovery Guide
output.meta_description: Your landlord won't return your deposit? Learn tenant rights, what landlords can deduct, and how to write a security deposit dispute letter that works.
content_outline: Landlord Withholding Security Deposit: Common Excuses and Illegal Tactics
Can Your Landlord Keep Your Security Deposit? Know the Legal Limits
What Can a Landlord Deduct? Separating Legitimate Charges from Normal Wear and Tear
Security Deposit Not Returned: State Deadlines and Your Tenant Rights
My Landlord Won't Give Me My Deposit Back: Immediate Steps to Take
What to Do When Your Landlord Doesn't Return Your Deposit: Demand Letters and Escalation
How to Write a Security Deposit Dispute Letter That Forces Action
key_points: Most states require landlords to return security deposits within 14 to 30 days and provide an itemized statement for any deductions; failure to do so often entitles tenants to the full deposit plus penalties
Landlords cannot legally deduct for normal wear and tear, routine cleaning, or pre-existing conditions, yet many withhold deposits unfairly hoping tenants won't fight back

A formal security deposit dispute letter citing state-specific landlord-tenant laws is the fastest, most effective first step to recover funds and is often required before filing in small claims court
DepositBack automates state-compliant demand letters that create legal urgency, helping frustrated tenants escalate from 'landlord not returning deposit' to refund received without hiring an attorney
output.cta: Don't let your landlord keep what's yours. Generate your state-specific security deposit demand letter in minutes and demand your deposit back today.
output.target_audience: Frustrated US renters whose landlord is withholding, delaying, or refusing to return a security deposit and need fast, legally sound recovery options
